risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A monitoring agency prepared the randomization list and provided sealed envelopes for treatment allocation." Comment: Although the paper does not name the exact random number method, the preparation of a randomization list by an independent monitoring agency, together with 1:1 random assignment, indicates a truly random allocation sequence rather than a predictable rule; there is no evidence of quasi-random allocation.

Quote: "A monitoring agency prepared the randomization list and provided sealed envelopes for treatment allocation." Comment: This explicitly reports a concealment mechanism using sealed envelopes. Although the paper does not state "opaque" or "sequentially numbered," the only direct description of allocation concealment indicates that assignment was hidden from recruiters via envelopes prepared by an independent monitoring agency, so I judge concealment as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This is a 1-year, prospective, controlled, randomized, rater blinded, parallel-group, monocentric pilot study" and "Included patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to continue monthly intravenous NTZ 300 mg or to de-escalate to every other day subcutaneous (s.c.) INFB 250 ug." Comment: The paper states that raters were blinded, but it does not report blinding of participants or treating personnel. Because the two interventions were clearly different (monthly IV natalizumab vs every-other-day SC interferon beta-1b), participants and personnel would very likely have known assignment. The main outcomes included relapses, disability progression, and MRI activity, which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of treatment allocation (e.g. symptom reporting, clinical management, adherence), so performance bias is at high risk overall.

Quote: "EDSS and relapses assessment was performed by an examining neurologist blinded to treatment." and "MRI disease activity was assessed via subtraction MRI (sMRI) and via count of Gd+L by an expert who was blinded to clinical data." Comment: The main clinical outcome assessor (neurologist) was explicitly blinded, and MRI outcomes were read by an expert blinded to clinical data. The outcomes assessed here (relapses/EDSS and MRI lesion counts) are therefore unlikely to have been influenced by knowledge of allocation.

Quote: "17/19 patients completed the study: one IFNB-patient withdrew consent (day 34) because she could not comply with study procedures; one NTZ-patient opted for an oral treatment (day 139). One IFNB-patient (#9) switched to rescue treatment at day 69 due to systemic side effects." Comment: Attrition was small overall (2/19 withdrew, ~11%) and appears reasonably balanced across groups, with reasons not obviously related to the main outcomes; the paper also states "Analysis was based on intention to treat," which supports low risk of attrition bias.
